worth_it_explanation: Attribution configuration in The Trade Desk (TTD) sits behind a few dropdowns in the Ad Group settings, but the defaults are generous to the platform and easy to leave untouched. A 30-day view-through window on a display campaign with a two-day conversion cycle is a common setup that makes the channel look stronger than it is. The check takes a few minutes per ad group and the conversation with the client about what the windows actually mean is worth having. A misconfigured attribution model does not prevent delivery, so the problem does not announce itself.
intro: The Trade Desk lets advertisers configure attribution at the ad group level: click-through and view-through lookback windows, and a last-touch or fractional attribution model applied within the platform's own reporting. These settings control which ad group gets credit for a conversion and how far back that credit reaches. The defaults favor longer windows, which can inflate reported performance for channels where the ad served but did not meaningfully drive the outcome. These checks confirm that the lookback windows fit the actual sales cycle, that view-through windows are not so long they credit display impressions that preceded the conversion by weeks, and that the chosen model reflects a deliberate decision rather than an untouched default.

audit_checks.title: Click-through and view-through lookback windows do not reflect the product's sales cycle
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Windows that run longer than the actual decision cycle absorb conversions from users who would have converted regardless of the ad, inflating reported return on ad spend (ROAS) and steering budget toward campaigns that appear to be driving outcomes they are not.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk records a conversion when a user who clicked or viewed an ad completes the tracked event within the lookback window set on the ad group. Click-through windows and view-through windows are configured separately. A click-through window of 30 days is standard in many platforms. A view-through window of 30 days on a display campaign means TTD will credit an ad group for any conversion that happens within a month of the user seeing an impression, regardless of what else the user did in between.

The window length matters because it determines which ad groups appear in the conversion report. A product with a two-day consideration cycle, such as a consumable or a low-cost impulse purchase, does not need a 30-day window. Setting one pulls in conversions from users who saw the ad weeks before they converted through a different path entirely. The campaign's reported cost per acquisition drops, ROAS rises, and the numbers justify more budget, but the attribution is capturing noise rather than signal.

A few signs the windows may be misaligned with the sales cycle:
  • The ad group reports a high view-through conversion rate on display campaigns alongside a low click-through conversion rate
  • The reported conversion volume in TTD is materially higher than what the Universal Pixel or a third-party measurement vendor reports for the same period
  • The window lengths have not changed since the campaign launched, and nobody on the team can explain why the current values were chosen
You may want to check the actual window values against the product category's typical decision timeline. For short-cycle products, a click-through window of seven days and a view-through window of one day are starting points worth evaluating. For high-consideration purchases such as software or B2B services, a longer click-through window is defensible but the view-through window should still be short.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a retail site, checkout data gives a clear view of how long customers take to convert after first exposure. You may want to pull average time-to-purchase from the analytics platform and compare it against the current lookback windows. If the window is three times the median path length, it is likely pulling in organic or direct conversions.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the form submission is the conversion, but the sales cycle extends past the form. A lead that submits today may have first been exposed to an ad four weeks ago. You may want to consider whether the lookback window is measuring ad-influenced leads or simply recording all leads that occurred in the window, regardless of whether the ad drove the intent.

educate.learn_more_note: The Trade Desk maintains its documentation for this topic in its partner portal, which requires a logged-in seat and is not publicly accessible. No public reference is linked here. The Trade Desk's free Edge Academy provides public training on its platform, and practitioners with platform access can find the specific guidance in the in-platform Help Center.

investigate.intro: Review the click-through and view-through window values on each active ad group and compare them against the product's typical consideration timeline.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign in the Kokai interface and navigate to the ad group you want to review.
In the ad group settings, locate the Attribution section. Note the click-through lookback window value and the view-through lookback window value.
Repeat for each active ad group. Flag any ad group where the view-through window exceeds the product's median consideration cycle, or where neither window has been changed from the platform default.

In TTD Reporting, pull a conversion report segmented by ad group and filter to view-through conversions only. Note whether any ad group's view-through conversion count is high relative to its click-through conversion count. A ratio where view-through conversions outnumber click-through conversions by more than two to one on a display-only ad group may point to windows that are too wide.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group, the click-through window, the view-through window, and the product's typical consideration cycle as estimated from available analytics data. An ad group where both windows are shorter than or equal to the consideration cycle passes. An ad group where either window materially exceeds the consideration cycle, or where the windows are at platform defaults with no documented rationale, is a fail recorded with the ad group name and current window values.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the window values and the product's consideration cycle estimate for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the click-through and view-through lookback windows on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk ad groups are not aligned with the product's actual sales cycle. Windows set wider than the typical decision timeline pull in conversions from users who converted through other paths, inflating the reported return on ad spend and directing budget toward activity that did not drive the outcome. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the window values on each active ad group, benchmarking them against the median time-to-conversion from the analytics platform, and setting view-through windows in particular to reflect the realistic window in which a seen impression could have influenced the decision.

audit_checks.title: View-through window is long enough to credit display impressions that had no role in the conversion
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A long view-through window is the most direct way a programmatic setup overstates its contribution. Display campaigns serve large impression volumes. A window of 14 or 30 days on a product with a 48-hour consideration cycle captures a high share of all conversions in the attribution window, making display look responsible for outcomes that were already in motion.

educate.base:
View-through attribution credits an ad group when a user who saw an impression, but did not click, converts within the view-through window. The logic is that display advertising builds awareness and influences decisions even when users do not click. That is true in some categories. It is also the mechanism that makes display numbers look strong when the window is long relative to the category's actual purchase pace.

The Trade Desk's default view-through window is 30 days on display and video ad groups. A 30-day view-through window on a display campaign targeting a broad audience will reach a large share of the addressable population in any given month. If enough of those users convert for any reason in the next 30 days, the ad group collects the attribution credit. The conversion could have happened through a branded search click, an email, or a repeat visit with no ad exposure after day one. The view-through window does not distinguish between these paths.

View-through attribution is not wrong in principle. Connected television (CTV) and audio campaigns, where clicking is not the expected response, depend on it as the primary measurement signal. The question is whether the window length matches the category and whether view-through conversions are being weighed appropriately against click-through conversions in optimization decisions.

A few signals that the view-through window may be inflating results:
  • Removing view-through conversions from the reporting row cuts the conversion count by more than half
  • The view-through conversion rate on display is higher than on search or paid social for the same conversion action
  • The campaign has not had its view-through window reviewed since the original setup
You may want to run a version of the report that separates view-through from click-through conversions and evaluate whether the view-through share is defensible given how the product sells.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: Retail advertisers with short purchase cycles, such as apparel, consumables, or event tickets, are particularly exposed to view-through inflation. A one-day view-through window is a reasonable starting point for these categories. You may want to compare the cost per acquisition with view-through included against the figure with view-through excluded to see the gap.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For lead generation, view-through attribution on display is harder to defend unless the product has a long consideration cycle and display is being used for awareness at the top of the funnel. You may want to consider whether the form submissions attributed to display view-throughs came from audiences the display campaign actually reached, or from audiences that were already in the pipeline from other channels.

investigate.intro: Pull conversion reporting segmented by attribution type, compare view-through and click-through volumes, and review the view-through window value on each ad group.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: In TTD Reporting (My Reports or the standard Reporting tab), build a report at the ad group level and include both click-through conversion and view-through conversion as separate columns.
For each display or video ad group, calculate view-through conversions as a share of total conversions. Note any ad group where view-through conversions account for more than 50 percent of the total.
Return to the ad group settings in Kokai and confirm the view-through window value. If the window is 14 days or longer on a short-cycle product, note it as a flag.

If a third-party measurement vendor such as DoubleVerify or Integral Ad Science (IAS) is active on the campaigns, compare the vendor's attributed conversion count to TTD's total conversion count for the same period. A large gap may point to the view-through window pulling in conversions the independent tool does not attribute to the ad.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per display or video ad group, the view-through conversion share (view-through conversions divided by total conversions) and the configured view-through window. An ad group where view-through conversions are less than 30 percent of the total and the window is at or below the product's consideration cycle passes. An ad group where view-through conversions exceed 50 percent of the total, or where the window exceeds the consideration cycle by a factor of two or more, is a fail recorded with the ad group name, view-through share, and current window value.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the view-through share and window value for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the view-through attribution window on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk display campaigns is long relative to the product's consideration cycle, and that view-through conversions account for a disproportionate share of the reported totals. This setup credits display impressions for conversions that completed through other paths within the window, which overstates the channel's contribution and can direct budget toward campaigns that are reaching audiences already on their way to converting. {{voice_recommend}} shortening the view-through window to a value that reflects the realistic window in which a seen impression could have influenced the decision, and pulling a segmented report that separates view-through from click-through conversions so the two signals can be evaluated independently.
